data.title: Stock index futures fall as rate cut talks continue; eyes on PCE report
data.description:
Stock index futures were lower on Friday, a day after the S&P 500 hit another record close, while traders braced for inflation data.

S&P 500 futures (SPX) -0.2%, Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) -0.2%, and Dow futures (INDU) -0.3%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was unchanged at 4.24%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) was flat at 3.65%.

Wall Street’s major averages finished higher on Thursday, as investors weighed a wave of economic data and Nvidia’s (NVDA) latest earnings, all as the S&P 500 surpassed 6,500 and marked yet another record close.

"Markets put in another decent performance yesterday, with the S&P 500 at a new record as positive data reassured investors about the near-term outlook. But even as equities hit new highs, investors remain heavily focused on the Federal Reserve’s independence, and today there’s going to be an emergency hearing about Lisa Cook’s position on the Board of Governors that’s due to start at 10am EST," Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen. 

In more rate cut news, Fed Governor Christopher Waller said Thursday he favors beginning U.S. interest rate cuts next month and anticipates additional cuts over the next three to six months, aiming to “ease monetary policy and move it to a more neutral stance.”

On the economic side, the July PCE report will land before the bell. Core PCE, which excludes food and energy prices, is expected to rise 0.3% M/M, according to the consensus of economists, unchanged from the 0.3% increase in June. On a year-over-year basis, though, the metric is seen rising 2.9% vs. 2.8% in the prior month, still above the Fed's 2% goal.

Economists estimate that the headline PCE number will rise 0.2% M/M vs. 0.3% in June and 2.6% Y/Y, unchanged from the previous month’s increase.

"The July personal income and spending data is due. The middle-income consumers’ relatively strong starting point for the year has helped to sustain consumption, although political polarization has created some distortions in spending. Consumption remains the key focus for overall economic activity in the U.S. this year," UBS' Paul Donovan said.

The International Trade in Goods will also come in before the market opens. The consensus forecast looks for the merchandise trade gap at $87.7 billion in June, versus $86.0 billion in June.

The August Chicago PMI will be released during market open. The Chicago PMI is seen ebbing to 45.2 in the month versus 47.1 in July.

data.title: Fed's Waller repeats call for 25-bp rate cut, sees more easing over next 3-6 months
data.description:
"Let's get on with it," Federal Reserve Governor Christopher Waller said while backing a 25-basis point interest rate cut at next month's monetary policy meeting, as the odds of further weakening in the labor market rise.

"I felt this way in July, and all the evidence since then has led me to feel more strongly about it today," he said at an event in Miami on Thursday. "While there are signs of a weakening labor market, I worry that conditions could deteriorate further and quite rapidly, and I think it is important that the FOMC not wait until such a deterioration is under way and risk falling behind the curve in setting appropriate monetary policy."

Waller was one of the two Fed governors that dissented from the central bank's decision to leave rates unchanged in July. For now, he doesn't think a cut greater than 25 bps is needed in September. 

"That view could change if the employment report for August points to a substantially weakening economy and inflation remains well contained," he cautioned, pointing to the nonfarm payrolls report due next Friday.

Waller said he doesn't believe that monetary policy has fallen substantially behind the curve. "… as I stand here today, I anticipate additional cuts over the next 3-6 months, and the pace of rate cuts will be driven by the incoming data."

The Fed official continues to expect tariffs to temporarily increase inflation. He added that current economic data support moving policy toward a neutral setting – that's 125 bps-150 bps lower than the current 4.25%-4.50% range.

data.title: Fed Governor Waller calls for 25 bps rate cut at September meeting
data.description:
Federal Reserve Governor Christopher Waller said Thursday he favors beginning U.S. interest rate cuts next month and anticipates additional cuts over the next three to six months, aiming to “ease monetary policy and move it to a more neutral stance.”

The U.S. central bank held its policy rate steady at the 4.25%-4.50% target range for a fifth straight meeting in July, despite President Donald Trump’s calls for lower borrowing costs. The Fed's next policy meeting is scheduled for September 16-17.

“I favored reducing the federal funds rate by 25 basis points at the FOMC's July meeting, and subsequent data on the labor market and inflation indicate this was the right call,” […] Based on what I know today, I would support a 25 basis point cut at the meeting on September 16 and 17,” Waller said Thursday.

“While there are signs of a weakening labor market, I worry that conditions could deteriorate further and quite rapidly, and I think it is important that the FOMC not wait until such a deterioration is under way and risk falling behind the curve in setting appropriate monetary policy,” he added.

Waller also said he did not think “a cut of larger than 25 basis points is needed in September.” Though he said that view could change if the employment report for August points to a substantially weakening economy, and inflation remains well contained.

data.title: Noah Holdings reports Q2 results
data.description:
• Noah Holdings press release (NYSE:NOAH): Q2
  • Revenue of $87.9M.
  • As of June 30, total assets under management stood at RMB 145.1 billion (US$20.3 billion), with USD-denominated assets under management up 7.4% to US$5.8 billion and USD-denominated assets under advisory rising 6.6% to US$9.1 billion, underscoring the success of the Company's product depth and global allocation capabilities.
